pattern: Every translation that aligns a set bit of one image onto a set bit of the other is captured by the offset between their coordinates, so tallying offsets across all pairs of set bits reveals the best shift in one sweep.
hints: A translation is fully described by how far you shift in rows and columns; for each candidate shift you only care how many set bits land on set bits.
Instead of trying every shift and re-scanning, look at each pair of a set bit in one image and a set bit in the other, and record the shift that would bring them together.

Collect the coordinates of the 1s in each image. For every such pair, the offset (r1 - r2, c1 - c2) is one vote; the most-voted offset is the answer. Count votes in a hash map and return the maximum, or 0 if there are no pairs.

edge_inputs.description: the canonical shift right and down by one
single empty cell yields no overlap
one image is entirely zero so overlap is zero
identical images overlap perfectly with no shift
disjoint single bits need a diagonal shift

stress.description: two large random binary matrices with a moderate density of set bits
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = min(30, max(1, size))
    img1 = [[1 if rng.random() < 0.4 else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    img2 = [[1 if rng.random() < 0.4 else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [img1, img2]
stress.note: size capped at 30 per the matrix bound
